essayHeadings: Born from Durga's Brow on the Battlefield
Where the Scriptures Speak of Her
Reading Her Fearsome Form
From Dakshina Kali to Mahakali
Sacred Nights and Days of Worship
The Stotra Tradition Around Kali
essay: She steps out of Durga's furrowed brow, dark and gaunt, garlanded with severed heads, and the demon army scatters. That is the moment the Devi Mahatmya gives us in its seventh chapter (Markandeya Purana, chapters 81 to 93), when Kali is summoned to cut down Chanda and Munda, earning the name Chamunda. Kali is not a softened goddess. She is Time itself, the force that devours what it creates. Devotees in Bengal and Assam know her less as terror and more as Ma, the mother who stands beyond fear because she has already swallowed death.

Her chief textual home is twofold. The Devi Mahatmya, embedded in the Markandeya Purana, places her on the cosmic battlefield. The Mahabhagavata Purana and the Kalika Purana, Tantric and devotional in tone, elaborate her myths and her ten Mahavidya forms, of which Kali is the first. The Karpuradi Stotra, a Tantric hymn of high antiquity, treats her name itself as a seed of liberation. Adi Shankaracharya, though a Vedantin, composed verses to the dark Mother. Across these texts she is worshipped as both the dissolver of the universe and the goddess who grants release to her devotees.

Look closely at the image. Four arms, sometimes ten. A garland of fifty severed heads, said to stand for the letters of the Sanskrit alphabet, so that speech itself hangs from her. A skirt of arms signifying the karma she severs. One hand holds a sword, another a freshly cut head; the remaining two offer protection and boons. Her tongue lolls out, her foot rests on the prone Shiva. That foot is not violence. The Mahabhagavata explains it as the instant she recognises her consort and halts, restraint in the middle of fury.

Kali wears many faces. Dakshina Kali, the gentlest, is the form most often installed in household and temple shrines, benign toward the devotee who approaches from the south. Bhadrakali is the auspicious protector. Shmashana Kali haunts the cremation ground, presiding over the truth of impermanence. Guhya Kali and Mahakali belong to deeper Tantric practice. In her Mahakali aspect she is the great dissolution at the end of time, lying beyond the gunas. The Kalika Purana and regional Tantric manuals preserve the meditation verses, the dhyana, that distinguish one form from the next.

Kali Puja falls on the new-moon night of Kartik, the Amavasya that coincides with Diwali in much of North India, but is kept as a distinct nocturnal worship in Bengal, Assam, and Odisha. Tuesdays and the dark Amavasya nights are held especially hers. The Tantric tradition favours midnight worship, when the boundary between worlds is thin. Navaratri, particularly its later nights, also honours her among the Mahavidyas. Animal-free offerings of hibiscus, the red flower sacred to her, and the chanting of her hymns mark these observances in homes that keep the older devotional calendar.

Her hymns are dense and old. The Karpuradi Stotra, attributed to the Tantric tradition, builds liberation out of her root mantra. The Kali Kavach, an armour-hymn, invokes her protection limb by limb. The Dakshina Kali Stotram and the Adya Stotra, drawn from the Brahmananda Purana lineage, are recited at her evening worship across Bengal. The hundred and eight and thousand names, the Kali Sahasranama, are chanted by Tantric practitioners. Shankaracharya's verses to the Mother round out a corpus that runs from fierce Tantric seed-syllables to tender lullabies sung at the feet of the dark goddess.

essayHeadings: The God Who Was Born to Slay Taraka
Skanda Purana and the Tamil Voice
The Spear, the Peacock, the Six Faces
Murugan, Subrahmanya, and the Six Abodes
Skanda Shashti and the Days of His Worship
Hymns to the Lord of the Vel
essay: A demon named Taraka had won a boon that only a son of Shiva could kill him, and the gods had no such son. From Shiva's seed, carried by Agni and the Ganga, nursed by the six Krittika stars, a child was born with six faces to drink from all six mothers at once. That is Kartikeya, the war-god the Devas crowned their general. He grew within days, took up the vel, and ended Taraka. The Skanda Purana, the largest of the eighteen Mahapuranas, is named for this very god.

His scriptural footprint is wide. The Skanda Purana carries his birth and campaigns across its vast recension. The Mahabharata's Aranyaka Parva narrates the same birth from Agni and the Krittikas. In the Tamil south he is Murugan, the most beloved of gods, sung in the Tirumurukatruppatai of the Pattuppattu anthology, one of the oldest sustained poems to a single deity in Tamil. The Kanda Puranam, Kachiyappa Sivachariyar's Tamil retelling of the Sanskrit Skanda lore, shaped centuries of devotion. North names him Kartikeya and Skanda; the south knows him as Murugan and Subrahmanya.

He rides a peacock, often shown with a serpent crushed beneath its claws, mastery over both vanity and time. His weapon is the vel, the spear his mother Parvati gave him, which split the mango-seed mountain Krauncha and pierced the demon Surapadman. Six faces, the Shanmukha, recall the six Krittika nurses and the six directions he commands. In the south he is frequently the unadorned youth holding the vel, sometimes flanked by his two consorts Valli and Devasena, the tribal girl and the Deva princess who together bind the wild and the celestial.

His forms map onto his shrines. The Arupadai Veedu, the six sacred abodes in Tamil Nadu, mark his life: Tiruparankunram, Tiruchendur where he defeated Surapadman, Palani where he is the renunciate Dandayudhapani, Swamimalai where he taught the meaning of Om to his own father, Tiruttani, and Pazhamudircholai. As Subrahmanya he is the perfect adept; as Dandayudhapani at Palani he is the bare ascetic who renounced the fruit-prize and circled his parents instead. Each form carries its own legend, preserved in the Kanda Puranam and the local sthala texts of each hill shrine.

Skanda Shashti is his great festival, six days in the Tamil month of Aippasi (October to November) recalling his six-day war on Surapadman, ending on the sixth lunar day with the Soorasamharam re-enactment at Tiruchendur. Devotees keep fasts across these six days. Thaipusam, on the Pusam star of the Tamil month Thai, draws enormous pilgrimages with kavadi-bearing. Vaikasi Visakam celebrates his birth-star. Tuesdays and the sixth lunar day, the Shashti, are kept for him through the year, often with milk-abhishekam and the chanting of his protective hymns.

The hymn tradition is rich and living. Adi Shankaracharya's Subrahmanya Bhujangam, composed at Tiruchendur, is a serpentine-metre outpouring sung for protection and grace. The Kanda Sashti Kavasam of Devaraya Swamigal, in Tamil, is among the most recited armour-hymns in the south, invoked daily by millions. The Skanda Shashti Kavacham, the Subrahmanya Ashtottaram, and the Shanmukha Stotram complete the daily liturgy. Arunagirinathar's Tiruppugazh, thousands of rhythmic Tamil verses, count among the supreme devotional achievements addressed to Murugan, sung still in temples across Tamil Nadu.

essayHeadings: Bursting from the Pillar at Twilight
The Bhagavata's Account of Prahlada
Man Above, Lion Below
Ugra, Yoga, and Lakshmi Narasimha
Narasimha Jayanti and His Sacred Days
The Kavacha and Karavalamba Hymns
essay: The tyrant Hiranyakashipu had asked, mockingly, whether his son's god was in the pillar of the hall, and struck it. The pillar split, and out of it came a form that was neither man nor beast, at the threshold of the doorway, in the dusk that is neither day nor night. Narasimha, the man-lion, tore the demon apart across his thighs with his claws, fulfilling every clause of a boon meant to make Hiranyakashipu deathless. The fourth avatara of Vishnu is the god of impossible exceptions, the protector who answers the cry of the helpless devotee.

The story belongs above all to the seventh book of the Bhagavata Purana, where the boy Prahlada, devoted to Vishnu against his father's will, survives poison, fire, and the trampling of elephants because his faith does not waver. The Narasimha Purana and the Vishnu Purana also carry the account, and the Harivamsa preserves an early form. The Bhagavata frames it not as a tale of violence but of bhakti vindicated, the child whose unshakeable love compels the Lord to break his own cosmic rules and appear from solid stone to save him.

His image is built on paradox. The face and mane are a lion's, the lower body a man's, the whole charged with fury. He is shown in mid-act, the demon Hiranyakashipu across his lap, intestines drawn out and worn as a garland, claws still buried. In the calmer Yoga Narasimha form he sits in meditation, a yoga-band wrapped around his knees, the rage stilled. Many arms hold the conch, discus, mace, and lotus of Vishnu alongside weapons of war. The form is deliberately liminal, refusing every category, the god who is both and neither.

Tradition counts many Narasimhas. Ugra Narasimha is the fierce slayer of the myth. Yoga Narasimha is the seated ascetic. Lakshmi Narasimha shows the goddess Lakshmi on his lap, cooling his anger after the kill, the form most often installed for household worship. Sthana Narasimha, Jwala Narasimha, and the nine forms of the Nava-Narasimha at Ahobilam in Andhra Pradesh, his foremost shrine, each mark a place in the legend. The Ahobilam Mahatmya in the Brahmanda Purana lineage preserves these nine, the most fearsome and the most gracious worshipped in adjoining hills.

Narasimha Jayanti falls on the fourteenth lunar day, the Chaturdashi, of the bright fortnight of Vaishakha (April to May), the evening hour matching the twilight of his appearance. Devotees fast through the day and break it only after sunset, when the avatara emerged. Swati nakshatra is held sacred to him. Saturdays are kept for Vishnu in his Narasimha form by many who seek protection from fear and enemies. The observance centres on his role as the swift defender, recited at dusk lamps lit at the threshold, the very boundary where he once appeared.

His hymns are armour for the frightened. The Narasimha Kavacham, ascribed to Prahlada himself in the Brahmanda Purana, is recited for protection limb by limb. The Lakshmi Narasimha Karavalamba Stotram of Adi Shankaracharya, with its refrain pleading for the lotus-hand of support, is among the most loved of his hymns. The Narasimha Stuti, the Divya Nama Kirtana, and the Sri Narasimha Ashtottaram fill the daily liturgy. The Kamasika Ashtakam and the verses of the Alvars in Tamil round out a tradition that treats his very name as a shield against terror.

essayHeadings: The Daughter of the Mountain Who Won Shiva
The Shiva Purana and Her Many Texts
Reading the Mother's Gentle Form
Sati, Gauri, Annapurna, and Durga
Teej, Gauri Puja, and Her Sacred Days
Stotras to the Mountain-Born Goddess
essay: She was born the daughter of Himavan, lord of the Himalaya, and she set her heart on Shiva the ascetic, who sat in meditation and would not look at her. So Parvati went to the mountains herself and performed penance so severe she gave up even leaves, earning the name Aparna, until Shiva relented and took her as his wife. That courtship, the union of the great renunciate and the goddess who refused to be refused, sits at the centre of Shaiva myth. Parvati is Shakti, the active power without which Shiva is inert.

Her chief text is the Shiva Purana, whose Rudra Samhita narrates her birth, her tapas, and her marriage in detail. The Devi Bhagavata Purana and the Markandeya Purana's Devi Mahatmya present her cosmic and martial forms. The Skanda Purana, the Kalika Purana, and the Lalita Sahasranama of the Brahmanda Purana expand her as the supreme goddess. Kalidasa's Kumarasambhava, the Sanskrit epic, immortalises her penance and wedding in verse. Across these she is at once the gentle consort of the householder myth and the absolute Devi from whom the universe unfolds.

In her benign form she is golden, two-armed or four, seated beside Shiva on Kailasa or holding the infant Ganesha and Kartikeya. As Gauri she is fair and serene, often shown with a lotus and a mirror, the very image of saubhagya, marital fortune. She may carry a rosary and a water-pot in her ascetic aspect, recalling her penance. Her vehicle is the lion she shares with Durga, or she stands without one as the domestic goddess. The iconography deliberately keeps her approachable, the mother and wife rather than the battlefield slayer of her fiercer selves.

She moves through a chain of forms. As Sati, her first birth, she immolated herself at Daksha's sacrifice when her father insulted Shiva, then was reborn as Parvati. As Gauri she is the fair bride; as Annapurna she holds the ladle and bowl, the goddess of food who once fed even Shiva when he begged at Kashi. As Durga and Kali she takes the field against demons. As Kamakshi, Meenakshi, and Lalita she presides over the great southern shrines. Each form is the same Shakti turned to a different work.

Her festivals cluster around marriage and devotion. Hartalika Teej and Hariyali Teej, in the monsoon month of Shravana and Bhadrapada, see women fast for marital welfare in her honour, recalling her own penance to win Shiva. Gangaur in Rajasthan celebrates Gauri across eighteen days. Navaratri honours her among the nine Durga forms. Mondays, sacred to Shiva, are kept for the divine couple, and the third lunar day, the Tritiya, is held hers. Married and unmarried women alike observe these vows, the vratas, taking Parvati as the model of steadfast devotion.

The hymns to her run from the tender to the philosophical. Adi Shankaracharya's Annapurna Stotram, sung at Kashi, pleads with the goddess of plenty for grace and food. His Soundarya Lahari, addressed to her as Lalita-Tripurasundari, is the supreme Sanskrit hymn to the Devi's beauty and power. The Parvati Vallabha Ashtakam, the Gauri Stotram, and the Lalita Sahasranama and Trishati form the core of her worship. The Devi Khadgamala and Shankara's Devi Aparadha Kshamapana Stotram, a prayer for forgiveness of a child's faults, complete a corpus sung by devotees across the subcontinent.

essayHeadings: The Sun Who Steadied Rama at Dawn
From the Surya Suktam to the Aditya Hridayam
Seven Horses and the Wheel of the Year
Aditya, Savitr, and the Twelve Suns
Ratha Saptami and the Days of the Sun
The Hymns Recited at Sunrise
essay: Rama stood exhausted on the battlefield, facing Ravana, when the sage Agastya came to him and taught him a hymn to the Sun, telling him to worship the lord of the sky before the fight. Rama recited it, drew strength from the rising disc, and went on to victory. That hymn is the Aditya Hridayam, and it appears in the Yuddha Kanda of Valmiki's Ramayana, in the hundred and fifth sarga. Surya is no abstraction in Hindu worship. He is the visible god, the one deity any devotee can see each morning.

The Sun is among the oldest gods of the Vedas. The Surya Suktam and the Rig Vedic hymns address him as the eye of Mitra and Varuna, the soul of all that moves and stands still. The Gayatri Mantra of the Rig Veda is itself a meditation on Savitr, the solar power that impels the mind. The Aditya Hridayam from the Ramayana, the Surya Ashtakam, and the solar passages of the Bhavishya Purana and the Brahma Purana carry his later worship. The Konark and Modhera temples were raised to him as visible scripture in stone.

He rides a chariot drawn by seven horses, driven by the legless charioteer Aruna, the dawn. The seven horses are read traditionally as the seven colours of light or the seven metres of the Veda. A single wheel, the wheel of the year with its twelve spokes for the months, turns beneath him. He holds two lotuses, one in each hand, and wears a crown of fire. His consorts are Sanjna and Chhaya, light and shadow. Cast in bronze or carved on temple walls, he is shown booted and erect, the only major deity regularly depicted wearing boots.

He is counted among the twelve Adityas, the solar deities born of Aditi, and in his full glory he is their sum. As Savitr he is the impeller invoked in the Gayatri. As Mitra he is the friend, as Pushan the nourisher, names the Veda gives to facets of the one Sun. The Adityas, listed in the Bhagavata and the Vishnu Purana, preside over the twelve months, so that each month has its own solar lord. Surya is also father, in the epics, of Yama, Yami, the Ashvins, Karna, and Sugriva, threading the solar line through the great stories.

Ratha Saptami, the seventh lunar day of the bright fortnight of Magha (January to February), marks the Sun turning his chariot toward the northern course and is kept as his birthday, observed with sunrise bathing and offerings of arghya, water poured toward the disc. Makar Sankranti, when the Sun enters Capricorn, celebrates the same northward turn. Chhath, in Bihar and the east, is the great four-day vrat of sunset and sunrise offerings standing in water. Sundays are his through the year, and dawn is his hour, when devotees offer water and the Gayatri.

His hymns are recited facing the rising sun. The Aditya Hridayam, taught by Agastya to Rama, is the foremost, chanted at dawn for strength and clarity. The Surya Kavacham guards the reciter limb by limb. The Surya Ashtakam, attributed to the Samba episode of the Bhavishya Purana, and the Surya Chalisa in the Hindi devotional tradition are sung daily. The Surya Sahasranama and the twelve names, the Dwadasa Nama, are murmured during the morning arghya. With the Surya Suktam and the Gayatri, these form the oldest continuous solar liturgy in the world, still offered each sunrise.
